sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Direct pressure is the most fundamental haemorrhage control technique. Combined with trauma/pressure bandages (e.g. Israeli Emergency Bandage, rolled gauze), it provides sustained compressive force to control compressible external haemorrhage in areas where tourniquets cannot be applied.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Direct pressure:**

1. Apply sterile dressing or clean cloth directly to wound

2. Apply firm, continuous pressure with flat hand and body weight

3. Maintain for minimum 5–10 minutes without lifting to check — lifting disrupts clot formation

**Israeli Emergency Bandage (Pressure Bandage):**

1. Place non-woven pad directly over wound

2. Wrap elastic bandage around limb/body part with moderate tension

3. Thread elastic through pressure bar applicator; pull back over bandage to create pressure point

4. Continue wrapping and secure with closure clip

**Wound packing (junctional / deep wounds):**

- Pack gauze into wound starting at bleeding source
- Apply firm pressure with fist for 3 minutes
- Secure with bandage

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not remove first dressing if soaked — add additional dressing on top
- Pelvic and abdominal wounds: direct pressure plus wound packing; transport immediately
- Haemostatic gauze preferred over standard gauze for junctional wounds
- Document: estimated blood loss, interventions, time of application
